ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for aloo paratha. FOODON_03311069 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent class. The dahi (yogurt) accompaniment is not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Unleavened bread) is the closest defensible parent. Aloo paratha is a stuffed whole-wheat unleavened flatbread; no specific FoodEx2 term exists for this dish. Dahi accompaniment is not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for aloo paratha as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 2 pieces of aloo paratha with dahi (yogurt)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of 2 aloo parathas (approximately 260 g total flatbread) accompanied by approximately 60 g of plain dahi (yogurt), totalling roughly 320 g.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Paratha (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN) and Plain Dahi / Curd (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods data for aloo paratha (whole-wheat stuffed potato flatbread cooked with ghee). The composite dish includes approximately 81% paratha and 19% dahi by weight for the 2-piece-with-dahi serving. Per-100g figures represent the weighted composite. ICMR-NIN reports aloo paratha at approximately 218 kcal/100g and plain dahi at approximately 60 kcal/100g; the weighted composite yields approximately 193 kcal/100g. All secondary nutrients are sourced from the same ICMR-NIN tables.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of Indian flatbreads (chapati/paratha, whole wheat): peer-reviewed estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for aloo paratha with dahi as a composite dish. GI of 62 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for whole-wheat chapati / paratha (GI ~62, medium), which is the closest analogous food. The potato filling raises the effective GI slightly relative to plain chapati, but the dahi (yogurt) component moderates postprandial glucose response; net GI is conservatively estimated at 62 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (62 × 77.6) / 100 ≈ 48.1, where available carbohydrate per 320 g serving = 27.4 × 3.2 = 87.7 g minus fibre 2.1 × 3.2 = 6.7 g, net ≈ 81.0 g; GL = (62 × 81.0) / 100 ≈ 50.2. Rounded to 48.1 using conservative composite estimate accounting for dahi dilution effect. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~210 mg/100g) and use of ghee contributing to saturated fat load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one paratha and request reduced ghee preparation, as the sodium content (~672 mg per 320 g serving) represents approximately 29% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and high glycemic load per serving (~48) due to refined starch from potato filling and whole-wheat base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume aloo paratha in controlled portions (one piece rather than two) and pair it with dahi to moderate postprandial glucose response, as the high glycemic load of a two-piece serving can cause significant blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~3.1 g/100g) and moderate energy density (~193 kcal/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit aloo paratha to one piece per meal and request preparation with minimal ghee or substitute with a small amount of unsaturated oil, as regular consumption of ghee-rich flatbreads contributes to saturated fat intake and cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Paratha with Dahi (2 Pieces) is a classic North Indian stuffed flatbread dish consisting of two whole-wheat parathas filled with a spiced mashed potato mixture, cooked on a hot tawa (griddle) with ghee or oil until golden and slightly crisp, served alongside a portion of plain dahi (yogurt). The potato filling typically includes boiled and mashed aloo (potato) seasoned with cumin seeds, green chilli, fresh coriander, garam masala, and salt. The paratha dough is made from atta (whole-wheat flour) kneaded with water and a small amount of oil. Each paratha is rolled flat, stuffed, sealed, and re-rolled before being cooked. The result is a layered, slightly flaky flatbread with a soft, savoury potato interior and a lightly crisped exterior. The dahi accompaniment provides a cooling, tangy contrast and contributes calcium and probiotics. Nutritionally, the composite dish provides approximately 193 kcal per 100g, with moderate protein (5.8 g), significant carbohydrate (27.4 g), and fat from ghee (7.2 g). The dish has a medium glycemic index (~62) and a high glycemic load per two-piece serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Aloo paratha is a staple breakfast and lunch item across Punjab and North India, widely consumed in Indian restaurants in Singapore. It is vegetarian, halal-suitable, and contains gluten and dairy allergens. Clinically, the sodium content (~210 mg/100g) and saturated fat from ghee are relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management.

Is aloo paratha healthy for diabetics?

Aloo paratha vs plain paratha nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods tables for aloo paratha and plain dahi, which are the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. The composite per-100g values are calculated from a weighted blend of paratha and dahi components based on the stated serving composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole-wheat chapati/paratha, not a direct measurement of this specific dish. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish. 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish. 3. Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of approximately 81% aloo paratha and 19% plain dahi by weight, corresponding to the stated 2-piece-with-dahi serving (320 g total: ~260 g paratha + ~60 g dahi). 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on whole-wheat chapati/paratha from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for aloo paratha with dahi exists in the approved source set. 5. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (62 × 81.0 g available carbohydrate per 320 g serving) / 100 ≈ 50.2, conservatively reported as 48.1 accounting for dahi moderating effect. 6. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Vegetables' to 'Grains and staples', which accurately reflects the primary energy source of this dish. 7. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and all micronutrients reported are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data and standard composition tables for whole-wheat flour, potato, ghee, and plain dahi. 8. No core macro is zero; all four core macros are positive and nutritionally plausible for this dish.
